exemplar.title: Investigation on Different Recall Methods and the Schema Theory

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigation on Different Recall Methods and the Schema Theory

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, operationalized aim linking recall type to schematic memory
Accurate description of schema theory with linkage to investigation
Explicit stating of null and research hypotheses with operationalized variables
weaknesses: Minor stylistic repetition in relevance explanation
Incomplete citation details for theory source
Directionality (one-tailed vs. two-tailed) not specified in hypothesis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction demonstrates strong performance overall: a precise, relevant aim is explained (strand 5-6 band), schema theory is described and linked to the study (5-6 band), and hypotheses are fully operationalized (6/6). However, minor stylistic repetition and incomplete citation details prevent the award of full marks. On balance, a score of 4/6 reflects secure top-band achievement with room for refinement.

strengths: Well-justified independent-measures design mitigating order effects
Convenience sampling described with participant characteristics and justification
Controlled variables clearly listed and several materials explained
weaknesses: Rationale for some controlled materials (e.g., the choice of 16 objects) is thin
Description of random allocation lacks detail
Not all environmental and procedural controls are fully detailed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section meets the criteria for an explained design (4/4) and sampling (4/4) but shows uneven justification in materials and controls (closer to the 3/4 band). Omissions in the rationale for object selection and the method of random allocation limit top-band performance. A score of 3/4 appropriately reflects solid description with some explanatory gaps.

strengths: Accurate calculation and reporting of descriptive statistics (mean, SD)
Appropriate use of Mann-Whitney U test with clear U, z, p values
Graphical presentation addresses the hypothesis with correctly labelled axes and error bars interpreted
weaknesses: Minor mislabelling of the Mann-Whitney U test as comparing means
Graph caption formatting issues and missing âGraph 1â label
Table headers misaligned and lacking units
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analysis and graphical presentation are appropriately chosen and largely error-free (5/6 band), and findings are interpreted in relation to the hypothesis (5/6 band). However, small conceptual and formatting errors prevent full marks. A score of 4/6 rewards accurate application while noting the need for precision in labeling and justification.

strengths: Discussion of findings linked to Brewer & Treyens and schema theory
Strengths and limitations are relevant and explained with regard to validity
Modifications are justified and directly address identified limitations
weaknesses: Findings discussion is accurate but relatively brief with limited alternative explanations
Some modifications (e.g., feasibility of morning testing) lack practical detail
Evaluation could deepen theoretical critique beyond procedural comparisons
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation section shows competent discussion of findings with reference to theory (4/6 band), thorough strengths and limitations with relevance (5/6 band), and well-justified modifications linked to limitations (6/6 band). Briefness in theoretical critique and some practicality gaps in modifications prevent the top mark. A score of 4/6 reflects strong but not exhaustive critical evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph with error bars is provided, axes are labelled and the graph visually addresses the hypothesis by comparing the two recall conditions.  Error bars are interpreted in the text.  Axis scaling and titles appear correct from the description, so it meets top-band expectations; a small stylistic overlap of title and axis prevents a perfect score, so 5 is assigned.

The student states the statistical outcome, links it to rejection of the null hypothesis, and interprets in terms of schema influencing memory.  Interpretation is explicit and clearly tied to both data pattern and hypothesis.  Depth is good, though not extensively nuanced, so 5 within the 5-6 band is justified.

Both null and research hypotheses are given.  The independent variable is operationalized as the presence vs. absence of an object list; the dependent variable is operationalized as the ânumber of correctly recalled schematic itemsâ.  Wording is precise and measurable, fully satisfying the 5-6 descriptor; no shortcomings are evident, so the full 6 is awarded.

Convenience sampling is named and reasons for its choice (cost, scheduling, familiarity) are provided.  Participant characteristics (age, gender, English proficiency, non-IB) are linked to reducing demand characteristics, showing explanation rather than description; thus the strand fits the 3-4 band at the top mark of 4.

Schema theory is described with key concepts (mental representations, encoding/storage/retrieval influence, types of schema) and the classic Brewer & Treyens study is outlined.  The student explicitly connects these ideas to the present investigation, explaining how a list may trigger participantsâ classroom schema.  The coverage is accurate and the link is explicit, meeting the top-band descriptor; minor textbook phrasing prevents a perfect 6, so 5 is appropriate.

Findings are compared to Brewer & Treyens and the role of schema is discussed, including how experimental modifications (real classroom, fewer recall modes) may have affected results.  The discussion is accurate but relatively brief and lacks deeper theoretical critique (e.g., alternative explanations), fitting the mid-band 3-4; the higher mark, 4, is merited.

The student states a clear aim â to investigate how the type of recall (free-written vs. written with a list) influences the number of schematic objects remembered â and then explains why this is relevant (implications for eyewitness testimony and memory reliability).  The explanation explicitly links everyday/legal importance to the study, moving beyond merely naming relevance.  Minor stylistic repetition is present but does not detract from an otherwise solid explanation; hence the work fits securely in the 5-6 band and is awarded 5.

Strengths (independent design, generalisability across grades, controlled environment) and limitations (all-female, small sample, grade imbalance, fatigue, visibility issues) are each explained with clear links to validity or generalisability.  Coverage is thorough though not exhaustively critical; thus a solid 5 in the top band is appropriate.

Controlled variables (time of day, same room, prohibition of talking, identical instructions) and materials (standardised script, 16-item list) are listed, with brief justification for several controls (minimise distractions).  While most choices are explained, the rationale for each material is thinner, so a solid but not highest performance within 3-4 is appropriate; 3 is awarded.

For each stated limitation the student proposes a directly linked modification (larger, more diverse sample; matched-pairs allocation; staggered observation; morning testing).  Each suggestion is justified in terms of how it would reduce the identified issue, demonstrating full alignment with the 5-6 descriptor.  The reasoning is explicit and convincing, earning the maximum 6.

The independent-measures design is not only described but justified (avoids order effects and demand characteristics).  Explanation of why the design suits the aim elevates it to the upper band; detail is adequate and accurate, so the top mark (4) within the 3-4 band is given.

Descriptive statistics (mean, SD) are correctly calculated; an appropriate non-parametric Mann-Whitney U is used for two independent samples of small size.  U, z and p are reported and decision rules stated accurately.  Minor justification mis-labels the test as comparing means, but computation appears sound; accuracy outweighs the small conceptual slip, warranting 5 in the 5-6 band.

annotations.comment: The evaluation acknowledges avoidance of order effects and demand characteristics, enhancing internal validity.
The title clearly conveys the investigationâs focus on recall methods and schema theory, framing the studyâs domain effectively.
The student could refine the title to specify the educational context or participant group, increasing precision and scope clarity.
The schema theory background provides a solid conceptual foundation, accurately describing encoding, storage, and retrieval processes.
The reference âOxford University Pressâ lacks author and publication date details; revise to complete the citation in APA format.
Consider previewing the studyâs variables in the background to better bridge theory with your investigationâs aim.
The aim is precisely operationalized, specifying both recall conditions and the measurable dependent variable.
Both the research and null hypotheses are explicitly stated, demonstrating clarity in expected outcomes.
Specify in the hypothesis whether a one-tailed or two-tailed test is intended to clarify directional expectations.
The independent measures design is well justified, highlighting how it mitigates order effects and demand characteristics.
Explain in more detail how random allocation was implementedâdescribe method or toolâto strengthen internal validity.
The controlled variables section is truncated; ensure all environmental and procedural controls are fully described.
Materials are systematically described, with separate sections for instructions and object lists enhancing clarity.
The rationale for selecting 16 objects lacks explanation on how they balance schematic versus non-schematic items.
Include justification for the total number of objects used (16) to demonstrate thoughtful material design.
Descriptive statistics are accurately calculated and presented in Table 1, facilitating clear comparison of conditions.
The table headers appear misaligned; adjust cell widths to improve readability and avoid confusion.
Add a unit label (e.g., ânumber of itemsâ) to the table heading to clarify what the means represent.
The bar graph compares means and error bars effectively, directly addressing the hypothesis visually.
The caption is split and unclear; consolidate the graph title and description into a single paragraph for clarity.
Include âGraph 1â label prominently beneath the image so it can be referenced easily in the narrative.
The inferential section mislabels the Mann-Whitney U test as comparing means. Clarify that it compares distributions of ranks.
Inferential results report U, z, and p-values clearly, enabling robust statistical interpretation.
The discussion effectively relates findings to Brewer & Treyens, highlighting how procedural differences may have influenced results.
The strengths section thoroughly links design choices to internal and external validity, demonstrating reflective evaluation.
Consider mentioning potential reactivity in single-session design, such as participants guessing the purpose over time.
Limitations are well articulated and each modification is directly justified, demonstrating thorough critical reflection.
The feasibility of morning testing to reduce fatigue deserves more detail (e.g., scheduling constraints, school hours).
When proposing a matched-pairs design, explain how pairing will be done to convincingly reduce participant variability.
The conclusion restates findings but could be strengthened by referencing effect size or significance level to contextualize impact.
The conclusion succinctly reaffirms how results support schema theory and the Brewer & Treyens findings.
The bibliography entry for ETSU lacks publisher location or retrieval date; standardize all references to APA style.
Ensure all online sources include access or retrieval dates and full URL formatting for consistency.
